blocks.content: The song ended. She didn't move.

The second time through she stopped pretending she was listening neutrally. She knew who the song was about. She knew who had put it there. She knew — had known for a long time, in the quiet careful place inside herself she didn't look at directly — what it meant that it was her name second on his list, that he'd remembered a song she mentioned on a walk in March, that he always came to find her, that the water tower had been theirs before it was anyone else's.
She was crying before she noticed she was.

Not quietly. Not in the careful, composed way she cried at movies or when something was manageable. Hard — the kind that comes up from somewhere low and doesn't ask permission. She pressed the back of her hand to her mouth. The ceiling fan turned. The song kept going.
When it ended she sat there in the quiet with her face wet and her chest aching and three years of something she'd been calling friendship pressing up against the inside of her ribs.
She wiped her face. Looked at the screen.
